svg.text: Cache Invalidation Flow — Atomic vs Eventual Consistency
Content update --> identify affected pages --> purge CDN --> purge reverse proxy --> purge app cache --> fragment invalidation, with rollback strategy
Complete Invalidation Pipeline — Step by Step
Example: Admin updates product description for product:P001 -- price changes from $99 to $79, new images added
Step 1: Identify All Affected Cache Keys
Using Surrogate-Key: product-P001 (one tag purge all)
Invalidation Complete — All layers serving fresh data
Atomic Invalidation vs Eventual Consistency — Two Approaches
Approach: Block serving until all layers purged
T+0ms: Mark content as "invalidating" -- serve 503 or stale
T+50ms: Mark "valid" -- serve fresh (app + Varnish ready)
- Users see clean transition: old or new, never mixed
- CDN still has eventual consistency at edge
Approach: Purge all layers asynchronously, accept stale window
T+0ms: Update content in DB, publish invalidation event
T+5ms: Parallel purge all layers (Redis, Varnish, CDN)
T+5-200ms: Some requests may hit stale cache (mixed content possible)
T+200ms: All layers converged, serving fresh data
During this window, CDN may serve old HTML with new app data
- Simple implementation: publish event, each layer subscribes
- Works with CDN's inherent eventual consistency
Rollback Strategy — When Invalidation Fails or New Content Has Bugs
Pro Tip: Keep previous version pre-cached with versioned keys
page:v2:product:P001 --> rollback to page:v1:product:P001 (instant, no DB revert needed)
1. Always version your cache keys (v1, v2, v3)
2. Keep previous version cached for instant rollback
3. Use feature flags to control new content visibility
4. Monitor 5xx rate, TTFB, and cache hit rate during deploys
